title: run: iptables/modprobe call in unprivileged context fails due to permissions
body:
NOTE: The full story and more details behind this are at https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-tracker/issues/1046.

In the context of Fedora CoreOS CI we do perform some **unprivileged** podman run.
One of them does the following:
```
$ podman network create testnetwork
$ podman run --rm -t --network=testnetwork registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora:35 getent hosts google.com
```

On current rawhide (F36) it started failing if the `ip_tables` module isn't already loaded.

What's interesting is that in this unprivileged context there is a call to `iptables` / `modprobe` which is failing with `Operation not permitted`. It seems to happen while `tearing down partially created network namespace`.

I'm unsure if the fallback/teardown is actually supposed to be performed this way for an unprivileged run.
Is this perhaps going by mistake through an execution flow that assumes it is running privileged?

For reference, the full error is
```
$ podman run --rm -t --network=testnetwork registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora:35 getent hosts google.com

ERRO[0000] error loading cached network config: network "testnetwork" not found in CNI cache 
WARN[0000] falling back to loading from existing plugins on disk 
ERRO[0000] Error tearing down partially created network namespace for container 539c41e10d9b5b62c020b71ed73891bf97c257e974928507dd1b967b33cb0125: error removing pod practical_lamport_practical_lamport from CNI network "testnetwork": running [/usr/sbin/iptables -t nat -D POSTROUTING -s 10.89.0.2 -j CNI-bd2745568755c29ef2bf4f1a -m comment --comment name: "testnetwork" id: "539c41e10d9b5b62c020b71ed73891bf97c257e974928507dd1b967b33cb0125" --wait]: exit status 3: modprobe: ERROR: could not insert 'ip_tables': Operation not permitted
                                                               iptables v1.8.7 (legacy): can't initialize iptables table `nat': Table does not exist (do you need to insmod?)
                Perhaps iptables or your kernel needs to be upgraded. 
Error: error configuring network namespace for container 539c41e10d9b5b62c020b71ed73891bf97c257e974928507dd1b967b33cb0125: error adding pod practical_lamport_practical_lamport to CNI network "testnetwork": failed to list chains: running [/usr/sbin/iptables -t nat -S --wait]: exit status 3: modprobe: ERROR: could not insert 'ip_tables': Operation not permitted
iptables v1.8.7 (legacy): can't initialize iptables table `nat': Table does not exist (do you need to insmod?)
Perhaps iptables or your kernel needs to be upgraded.
```

comments.body: @Luap99 Looks like CNI is trying to run the IPTables code despite being rootless?

This is expected, normally iptables will load the necessary modules for you but this does not work as rootless user. You either have to load the kernel modules manually as root before or you cannot use networks and have to run with slirp4netns.

There might be some middle ground here when thinking about this from a UX perspective. @Luap99 what you said is true about the rootless user not really being able to control this, but `systemd` upstream [just changed some default behavior](https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-tracker/issues/1046#issuecomment-996053567) and you're probably going to have a lot more people bumping up into this limitation.

Maybe podman could provide a service that an admin could enable that would load the iptables module. So you could tell users when they hit this problem they need to `sudo systemctl enable podman-iptables-module.service`. Maybe the error message could even have that information in it. Or maybe we'd want to make it on by default? Not sure, just throwing out ideas.
Either way someone has to figure out which modules are actually needed, it is definitely more than just `ip_tables`.
@Luap99 - anything more we can do here? Should this be re-opened? Should the conversation move somewhere else?

> Loading modules by default on boot makes sense too me when other applications no longer load it for us. I am not sure if we need a systemd unit, adding the needed modules to /etc/modules-load.d should work too.

I think we (and generally all distros) would be happy if podman package could carry a fragment doing that.
A `/usr/lib/modules-load.d/podman-network-iptables.conf` would fit nicely,
Can you please re-open this ticket and push in that direction?
I think the main point is that someone has to verify which modules are needed right now. This will be different depending on if your system uses `iptables-legacy` or `iptables-nft`.

I went back and looked at `rawhide` (`36.20211210.91.0`) from before our tests started failing. Here is what is loaded before and after `podman` was able to load the kernel modules:

```
[core@cosa-devsh ~]$ iptables --version
iptables v1.8.7 (legacy)
[core@cosa-devsh ~]$ podman network create testnetwork
/var/home/core/.config/cni/net.d/testnetwork.conflist
[core@cosa-devsh ~]$ lsmod | grep table
ip_tables              36864  0
[core@cosa-devsh ~]$ podman run --rm -t --network=testnetwork registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora:35 getent hosts google.com
Trying to pull registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora:35...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob c6183d119aa8 done  
Copying config e417cd49a8 done  
Writing manifest to image destination
Storing signatures
2607:f8b0:4009:801::200e google.com
[core@cosa-devsh ~]$ lsmod | grep table
iptable_filter         16384  0
iptable_nat            16384  0
nf_nat                126976  2 iptable_nat,xt_MASQUERADE
ip_tables              36864  2 iptable_filter,iptable_nat
```




If I do this same test on the same system but with iptables-nft here is what I see:

```
[core@cosa-devsh ~]$ iptables --version
iptables v1.8.7 (nf_tables)
[core@cosa-devsh ~]$
[core@cosa-devsh ~]$ lsmod | grep table
ip_tables              36864  0
[core@cosa-devsh ~]$ podman network create testnetwork
/var/home/core/.config/cni/net.d/testnetwork.conflist
[core@cosa-devsh ~]$ podman run --rm -t --network=testnetwork registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora:35 getent hosts google.com
Trying to pull registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora:35...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob c6183d119aa8 done
Copying config e417cd49a8 done
Writing manifest to image destination
Storing signatures
2607:f8b0:4009:803::200e google.com
[core@cosa-devsh ~]$ lsmod | grep table
nf_tables             290816  3 nft_compat,nft_counter,nft_chain_nat
nfnetlink              24576  2 nft_compat,nf_tables
ip_tables              36864  0
```

@dustymabe Thanks that is is very helpful

@baude @mheon WDYT? Just add the modules to a config file and ship it via the podman rpm?
Seems sensible. I'm a little worried about loading nftables on systems that only use iptables (or vice versa, when we get full nftables support into Netavark) but it shouldn't be a big deal.

> I just installed f36 and default is iptables-nft where it will work out of the box. It looks like the nf_tables module can be loaded by an unprivileged user so we do not have to add this explicitly. 

Interesting. I did not know that. Do you know what causes a module to require privilege versus not?

> @lucab @dustymabe Is there any reason why FCOS uses iptables-legacy in the first place?

Up until this point we've defaulted to iptables-legacy but we'll be migrating people by default with the switch to F36. 
> 
> Since old upgrades might still use iptables-legacy I will add the `ip_tables` and `ip6_tables` modules to the list but I am not sure if we want to keep this is the long run.

We do offer people the option to stay on iptables-legacy, though, so the above is appreciated for a while at least.

> > I just installed f36 and default is iptables-nft where it will work out of the box. It looks like the nf_tables module can be loaded by an unprivileged user so we do not have to add this explicitly.
> 
> Interesting. I did not know that. Do you know what causes a module to require privilege versus not?

I don't know. I just tested it with f36 and check the nf_tables was not loaded before I run the podman command with iptables-legacy.



> > Since old upgrades might still use iptables-legacy I will add the `ip_tables` and `ip6_tables` modules to the list but I am not sure if we want to keep this is the long run.
> 
> We do offer people the option to stay on iptables-legacy, though, so the above is appreciated for a while at least.

Yes we should keep it for a while until we know that almost all users have iptables-nft.
